frameworks.name: online psychophysics methodology
frameworks.one_line: browser-based method to estimate viewing distance for stimulus-size control

frameworks.name: blind-spot localization
frameworks.one_line: physiological landmark used to compute viewing distance in-browser

frameworks.name: visual crowding
frameworks.one_line: validation task requiring precise visual-angle calculation

core_question: Can a browser-based virtual chinrest enable controlled psychophysics in uncontrolled online environments?
key_assumptions: blind-spot location provides a reliable geometric anchor for viewing distance
stimulus size in visual angle can be derived from estimated viewing distance
participants can comply with simple browser-based calibration steps
manipulations: viewing distance varied in laboratory validation
display size varied in laboratory validation
participant population (young children, older adults, dyslexic readers)
key_findings: average viewing-distance estimation error of about 3.25 cm
online crowding measures replicated laboratory crowding effects
crowding increases with eccentricity online as in the lab
young children, older adults, and dyslexics show elevated crowding
interpretations: the virtual chinrest enables visual-angle-dependent psychophysics on the web
large-scale online psychophysics can recover classical lab effects across populations
